attributes.stem:
A 28-year-old woman presents to the General Practice clinic for follow-up after receiving treatment for presumed pelvic inflammatory disease ten days ago. She initially presented with one week of pelvic pain and increased vaginal discharge. Initial examination revealed bilateral adnexal tenderness, cervical excitation and a moderate amount of thick, white vaginal discharge. She received the appropriate treatment and reports having completed the courses of antibiotics.

She has no significant medical history, has an etonogestrel implant for contraception and has no plans for conceiving in the next few years. On examination today, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Abdominal examination reveals no tenderness or masses.

Vaginal swab for microscopy, culture and sensitivity showed normal vaginal flora, and endocervical swabs for chlamydia, gonorrhoea and trichomoniasis were negative. A pelvic ultrasound was ordered as part of the initial work up and the results show a 37 x 24mm complex cystic structure on the left side that likely represents a hydrosalpinx. She completed treatment with the appropriate antibiotics.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
options: Reassurance and advise that she has completed treatment
attributes.explanation:
This case presents a scenario of a newly discovered hydrosalpinx in a woman who has recently been treated for pelvic inflammatory disease (PID). A hydrosalpinx is a fluid-filled distension of the fallopian tube, often a sequela of PID. The most appropriate management in this case is to repeat ultrasound in six weeks. Follow-up imaging is recommended to monitor for potential resolution or progression which may indicate a diagnosis of endometriosis. Were she to be older, referral for further investigation may be recommended as this finding may represent a malignancy.

PID is often associated with common sexually transmitted infections (eg. Chlamydia trachomatis, Neisseria Gonorrhoea and Trichomonas vaginalis), however there are multiple other pathogens that can be responsible. The recommended treatment for patients with mild to moderate PID is intramuscular ceftriaxone, oral doxycycline and oral metronidazole which provides broad spectrum coverage. Pathogens associated with bacterial vaginosis, or those typically found in the gastrointestinal or respiratory tract have also been identified in cases of PID, including Escherichia coli and Haemophilus influenzae. These pathogens are commonly not seen on swabs, but are covered by the broad spectrum antibiotics given as treatment of PID. Mycoplasma genitalium can cause PID less commonly, but identification is important as treatment differs to that of usual PID, with moxifloxacin being the recommended agent.

Repeating the collection of swabs is unnecessary as the patient has already completed appropriate treatment for PID. Furthermore, it is unlikely that swabs would identify any pathogen as she would have only completed her course of antibiotics three days ago. There is no pathogen identified in up to one third of patients with PID, so negative swabs is not an uncommon finding. The presence of a hydrosalpinx does not itself indicate ongoing active infection, and given she has responded to treatment and is currently asymptomatic, further testing at this stage is not indicated.

Referral for hysterosalpingogram is not indicated at this stage. A hysterosalpingogram is a radiographic procedure used to assess the patency of fallopian tubes, often in the context of investigating infertility. It is not necessary for this patient at this time, however it may be considered in the future should she have difficulty conceiving.

Reassurance and advising that she has completed treatment is insufficient management. While it's true that she has completed the appropriate antibiotic course, the incidental finding of a hydrosalpinx requires follow-up to ensure proper management.

Repeat course of broad-spectrum antibiotics is not necessary as the patient has already completed treatment for PID and is currently asymptomatic. The presence of a hydrosalpinx does not itself indicate active infection requiring further antibiotic treatment.

The key in managing incidental findings like this is to provide appropriate follow-up without over-treatment. Repeating the ultrasound in six weeks allows for monitoring of the condition while avoiding unnecessary interventions. The patient should be advised to return sooner if she develops symptoms. If the hydrosalpinx persists on follow-up imaging, further management decisions can be made based on the patient's symptoms, fertility desires, and the characteristics of the hydrosalpinx.
learningPoints: Hydrosalpinx is a fluid-filled distension of the fallopian tube that can be a complication of pelvic inflammatory disease.
Follow-up imaging is recommended for newly diagnosed hydrosalpinx to monitor for resolution or progression.
references: Mitchell CM, Anyalechi GE, Cohen CR, Haggerty CL, Manhart LE, Hillier SL. Etiology and Diagnosis of Pelvic Inflammatory Disease: Looking Beyond Gonorrhea and Chlamydia. J Infect Dis. 2021 Aug 16;224(12 Suppl 2):S29-S35. doi: 10.1093/infdis/jiab067. PMID: 34396407; PMCID: PMC8365120. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC8365120/

El-Kharoubi AF. Tubal Pathologies and Fertility Outcomes: A Review. Cureus. 2023 May 11;15(5):e38881. doi: 10.7759/cureus.38881. PMID: 37197301; PMCID: PMC10184952. Available at: https://pmc.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/articles/PMC10184952/
Juganavar A, Joshi KS. Chronic Pelvic Pain: A Comprehensive Review. Cureus 2022; 14(10): e30691. DOI 10.7759/cureus.30691
